claim: Rejection census: 20 distinct rejection events across 16 unique manuscript families in tex/submitted/submission_log.txt as of 2026-05-13, plus 2 author-side withdrawals (Indagationes Item 20; J.Adv.Res. Item 25 pre-submit).
Desk-screen majority: 17 of 20 rejections (85%) returned no substantive content critique; 15 of 20 (75%) had ≤10-day turnaround consistent with desk-screen rather than refereeing.
Single-venue concentration: 8 of 28 journal submissions (29%) went to Experimental Mathematics before the project-blacklist threshold was reached at desk-reject n=7 (Item 6, Kasprzyk, 2026-04-25).

Most-frequent rejection-reasoning class: Class B content-mute desk at 35% (7 of 20). Other classes: F volume-fatigue 40% (8 of 20 if ExpMath cluster counted as F not B; B drops to 35% under that convention), A venue-state-backlog 15% (3 of 20), C policy-disclosed 10% (2 of 20), D scope-fit 10% (2 of 20), E qualification-gap 5% (1 of 20).

Framing-hypothesis test result: the 2026-05-12 corpus review hypothesis 'the math is fine but the framing makes it hard to review' explains 12 of 20 rejections (60%) cleanly; remaining 40% split between venue-state-uncontrollable (15%, 3 of 20), manuscript-substance-policy (10%, 2 of 20), and inconclusive operator-side errors / board-policy (15%, 3 of 20).
